title: Health psychology: determinants of health
summary: The option opens with the question that reframes all of modern health science: why do two people exposed to the same virus, the same surgery or the same stressful year end up in such different health? This lesson builds the biopsychosocial model, sorts the determinants of health into risk and protective factors, distinguishes biological, cognitive and sociocultural influences on health behaviour, contrasts dispositional and situational explanations, and shows how stress, addiction and lifestyle turn these factors into physical outcomes — all anchored to named studies you can USE, not just describe, in a Paper 2 essay.
learningObjectives: Explain the biopsychosocial model as three interacting factors, and contrast it with the biomedical model.
Classify determinants of health as risk factors or protective factors, and explain how they raise or lower vulnerability.
Distinguish biological, cognitive and sociocultural determinants of health behaviour, and dispositional from situational explanations.
Explain how stress, addiction and lifestyle translate these determinants into physical health outcomes.
Use and evaluate named supporting studies to build a top-band Paper 2 essay, rather than merely describing them.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Health is a three-way conversation
simpleExplanation.summary: Whether you stay well or get ill is rarely decided by one thing. Your biology, your mind and your social world are constantly talking to each other, and health is the outcome of that conversation — not of any single voice in it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a house catching fire. The biomedical model looks only at the spark (the pathogen). The biopsychosocial model asks the fuller question a fire investigator asks: was there a spark (biology), was the house full of dry timber because the owner never cleared it (behaviour driven by beliefs and stress), and was it built in a tinder-dry, poorly served neighbourhood far from a fire station (social environment)? Change any one of those and the outcome changes. Health works the same way — the spark alone rarely tells the whole story.
steps: Name the health outcome you are explaining (e.g. recovery from surgery, susceptibility to infection, developing an addiction).
Identify determinants across all three domains — biological, psychological/cognitive, and sociocultural — and label each as a risk or a protective factor.
Explain the mechanism that links the factor to the body (e.g. how chronic stress raises cortisol and suppresses immune function).
USE a named study as evidence for that mechanism — state its aim, procedure and findings, then say what the finding proves about your argument.
Evaluate: methodology, correlation versus causation, competing dispositional versus situational explanations, and cultural or individual variation.

flashcards.back: A model in which health and illness result from the interaction of THREE factors — biological, psychological and social — none of which is sufficient alone. The key word is 'interaction': the factors modify each other, they are not just added together.

The older view that illness is a purely biological malfunction (pathogen, injury, genetic fault), treating mind and body as separate. It explains acute infectious disease well but struggles with chronic, lifestyle-related illness.
The full range of factors that push an individual toward wellness or illness — biological, cognitive/psychological and sociocultural. They act as either risk factors or protective factors.

A RISK factor increases the probability of poor health (e.g. chronic stress, smoking, low SES, social isolation). A PROTECTIVE factor decreases it or buffers harm (e.g. social support, optimism, self-efficacy, exercise). The same variable can be one or the other depending on direction.
Stable INTERNAL characteristics of the person — optimism, self-efficacy, resilience, personality — assumed to be relatively consistent across situations.
EXTERNAL features of the environment or context — stressors, drug availability, socioeconomic conditions, social norms — that shape health behaviour regardless of personality.

A person's belief in their capacity to carry out the behaviours needed for a given outcome. A cognitive determinant: high self-efficacy predicts better adherence to health advice and successful behaviour change.
The claim that social support matters most under stress: it 'buffers' the individual, reducing the physiological impact of stressors on health rather than helping equally at all times.

Chronic stress activates the HPA axis, raising cortisol; sustained cortisol suppresses immune activity, increasing susceptibility to infection and slowing healing. This is the mechanism that links a psychosocial factor to a biological outcome.

Addiction reflects biological vulnerability (dopamine reward, genetic predisposition), cognitive factors (expectancies, coping beliefs) AND situational factors (availability, stress, social environment) — a textbook case where no single domain explains the outcome.

Most determinants-of-health data are correlational. Optimists being healthier does not prove optimism CAUSES health — a third variable such as SES may drive both. Say what the study can and cannot establish.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: describe vs USE research
flashcards.back: Describing a study earns partial credit at best. USING it means explicitly stating what its finding demonstrates about YOUR argument ('this shows that...'). Use, not description, is what lifts Criterion C into the top band.

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'The biopsychosocial model says health is caused by biology plus some social factors.' The main flaw is that it:
options: Omits that the three factors (biological, psychological AND social) INTERACT, not simply add together
Should not mention biology at all
quickCheck.answer: Omits that the three factors (biological, psychological AND social) INTERACT, not simply add together

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is a PROTECTIVE factor rather than a risk factor for health?
A person's optimism and belief in their own ability to change (self-efficacy) are best classified as:
In Robins et al.'s study of returning Vietnam veterans, most who had used heroin in Vietnam did not relapse back home. This best supports a ______ explanation of addiction.
options: Purely dispositional (it is all in the personality)
Situational (environment and availability strongly shape the behaviour)
quickCheck.answer: Situational (environment and availability strongly shape the behaviour)

quickCheck.prompt: An essay states Cohen et al.'s aim, procedure and findings in full but never says what the finding proves about the argument. Under our marking, this most limits:
options: Criterion C (Use of research) — the study is described, not USED
quickCheck.answer: Criterion C (Use of research) — the study is described, not USED

faq.q: For a 22-mark essay, how many studies do I actually need?
faq.a: Two, used thoroughly, is the sweet spot. Our markband caps Use of research (Criterion C) when only one relevant study appears, so a single study — however well described — cannot reach the top band. But three studies mentioned in passing is worse than two studies whose findings are explicitly linked to your argument. Aim for two named studies you can genuinely USE, plus a critical point drawn from each.

faq.q: What is the difference between a determinant of health and a determinant of health BEHAVIOUR?
faq.a: A determinant of health can act directly on the body (e.g. a virus, or cortisol suppressing immunity). A determinant of health BEHAVIOUR shapes what the person DOES — whether they smoke, exercise, adhere to treatment or seek help. Health behaviours are themselves grouped as biological (e.g. genetic reward sensitivity), cognitive (e.g. self-efficacy, risk perception) and sociocultural (e.g. peer norms, SES). Many determinants affect health both directly and indirectly through behaviour.

faq.q: Is 'dispositional versus situational' just the same as 'nature versus nurture'?
faq.a: They overlap but are not identical. Nature/nurture is about the ORIGIN of a trait (genes versus environment over development). Dispositional/situational is about the immediate CAUSE of a behaviour: is it best explained by something stable inside the person (disposition) or by the current context (situation)? Robins' Vietnam findings are the classic reminder that a behaviour which looks dispositional (addiction) can be strongly situational.

faq.q: Can I use the same study for knowledge (B) and for research (C)?
faq.a: Yes — a well-chosen study contributes to both, and that is efficient. State the psychology (the mechanism, the model) to earn Criterion B, then present the study's aim/procedure/findings and say what it demonstrates to earn Criterion C, then critique it to earn Criterion D. One study, worked properly, can feed three criteria.

sections.content: Two colleagues share an office through a brutal, high-pressure winter. Both catch the same circulating virus. One shrugs it off in a day; the other is floored for a fortnight and develops a chest infection. The biomedical model — illness as a purely biological event — cannot explain the difference, because the pathogen was the same. Health psychology can. This lesson is about the determinants of health: the biological, psychological and social factors that decide which way the balance tips. We will build the biopsychosocial model, sort determinants into risk and protective factors, separate the influences on health BEHAVIOUR, weigh dispositional against situational explanations, and trace how stress, addiction and lifestyle convert all of this into physical outcomes — with named studies you can use in an essay.
From biomedical to biopsychosocial

For most of medical history the biomedical model dominated: illness was a malfunction of the body's biological systems, caused by pathogens, injury or genetic fault, and the mind was treated as separate from the body. This model was spectacularly successful against acute infectious disease. But it explains chronic, lifestyle-linked illness — heart disease, type 2 diabetes, many cancers — poorly, because those outcomes depend on how people live, feel and are situated, not just on their cells.

The biopsychosocial model replaces this with three interacting factors. BIOLOGICAL: genes, immune function, neurochemistry. PSYCHOLOGICAL (cognitive/behavioural): beliefs, stress appraisals, coping style, health behaviours. SOCIAL: relationships, socioeconomic status, culture and norms. The single most examined idea here is the word INTERACTING. The model is not 'biology plus a bit of social context bolted on'. Each factor modifies the others: social stress alters biology (cortisol, immunity); biological vulnerability shapes psychological coping; cultural norms determine which behaviours are even available. Miss the interaction and you have merely re-described the biomedical model with extra words.

items: **Biomedical model** — illness = biological malfunction; mind and body separate; strong for acute infection, weak for chronic lifestyle disease.
**Biopsychosocial model** — health = the INTERACTION of biological, psychological and social factors.
**The examinable core** — the factors interact and modify one another; they are not simply added together.
**Consequence for treatment** — it justifies interventions that target behaviour and social context, not only the body (e.g. stress management, social support, tackling socioeconomic inequality).

sections.content: Risk factors and protective factors

It helps to sort determinants by the direction of their effect. A RISK factor raises the probability of poor health; a PROTECTIVE factor lowers it, or buffers the person against harm they are already exposed to. Crucially, the two are mirror images across the same dimensions, and a single variable can appear on either side depending on its level: strong social ties are protective, social isolation is a risk factor; optimism protects, hostility and chronic pessimism put you at risk.

items: **Biological risk / protective** — genetic predisposition, family history (risk); robust immune function, genetic resilience (protective).
**Cognitive / dispositional** — chronic stress appraisal, low self-efficacy, hostility (risk); optimism, high self-efficacy, adaptive coping (protective).
**Sociocultural / situational** — low socioeconomic status, social isolation, harmful norms (risk); high social support, high SES, health-promoting norms (protective).
**Behavioural** — smoking, poor diet, physical inactivity, harmful drinking (risk); exercise, adherence to treatment, help-seeking (protective).
**Direction matters** — the same dimension supplies both a risk and a protective pole; naming which pole you mean is what earns marks.

sections.content: Determinants of health BEHAVIOUR: biological, cognitive, sociocultural

Many determinants act on health indirectly, by shaping what people DO. Health behaviour — smoking, exercising, adhering to treatment, seeking help — is itself driven by factors from all three domains, and a good essay keeps them distinct.

items: **Biological determinants** — genetic predispositions and neurochemistry. Individual differences in dopamine reward sensitivity, for example, make some people more likely to repeat rewarding but harmful behaviours such as drug use or overeating.

**Cognitive determinants** — beliefs and appraisals. Self-efficacy (belief you CAN change), perceived risk (belief the threat applies to you), and outcome expectancies all predict whether a person starts and sustains a health behaviour. Low perceived risk is why many people ignore good advice.

**Sociocultural determinants** — the social environment. Peer and family norms model behaviour; socioeconomic status shapes what is affordable and accessible; cultural stigma (e.g. around mental illness) suppresses help-seeking. We tend to do what those around us do, and what our circumstances permit.

sections.content: Dispositional versus situational determinants

A recurring debate cuts across all of the above: is a health behaviour better explained by something stable INSIDE the person (a disposition) or by the person's CONTEXT (the situation)? Dispositional explanations point to optimism, self-efficacy, personality and resilience. Situational explanations point to stress, availability, socioeconomic conditions and norms. The honest answer is usually 'both, interacting' — but the debate is a rich source of critical thinking, because our intuition heavily over-weights disposition (the fundamental attribution error applied to health).

sections.question: Worked example 1 — Explain, using one study, the difference between dispositional and situational determinants of a health behaviour. (short-answer style, ~9 marks)
sections.solution:
**Set up the distinction.** Dispositional determinants are stable internal characteristics of the person (personality, optimism, self-efficacy); situational determinants are external features of the environment (availability, stress, social context). Addiction is a useful test case because it is intuitively seen as dispositional ('an addictive personality') yet is strongly shaped by situation.

**Use a study — Robins et al. (1974).**
- *Aim:* to investigate drug use in US soldiers returning from Vietnam, where heroin use had been common.
- *Procedure:* returning veterans were surveyed and tested for drug use in Vietnam and again after returning home to the very different US environment.
- *Findings:* although a large proportion had used heroin in Vietnam, the great majority did NOT continue or relapse once home; relapse rates were far lower than expected for heroin dependence.

**Use the finding (do not just report it).** If addiction were purely dispositional, changing the environment should not matter — the 'addictive personality' would follow the person home. It did not. The dramatic drop when the SITUATION changed (removal from a war zone, loss of easy availability, return to normal roles) demonstrates that situational determinants powerfully shape a behaviour we usually attribute to disposition.

**Balance and conclude.** This does not erase disposition — the minority who did relapse may have had greater biological or dispositional vulnerability, and self-report over such a sensitive topic is a limitation. The defensible conclusion is that health behaviour is best explained by dispositional and situational factors INTERACTING, with the study a warning against over-attributing behaviour to personality alone.

sections.content: How stress, addiction and lifestyle become physical health
The determinants above only matter because they eventually reach the body. Three well-understood routes do that work.

items: **Stress** — a chronic stressor activates the HPA axis and sustains cortisol; prolonged cortisol suppresses immune activity, increasing susceptibility to infection and slowing wound healing. This is the mechanism that turns a psychosocial event into a measurable biological outcome, and it is the pathway most worth learning precisely.

**Addiction** — repeated use of a substance recruits the dopamine reward system, drives tolerance and withdrawal, and — via the biopsychosocial route above — is initiated and maintained by biological vulnerability, cognitive expectancies AND situational availability together.

**Lifestyle** — diet, physical activity, smoking and drinking are behaviours, but they accumulate into biological risk (arterial damage, insulin resistance, carcinogen exposure). They are the clearest case of psychological and social determinants writing themselves onto the body over years.

sections.content: Two named studies let you evidence these routes. Scheier & Carver (1985) followed 51 male coronary artery bypass patients, measuring dispositional optimism before surgery; optimists showed faster recovery and better quality of life six months on, consistent with optimism promoting adaptive, problem-focused coping and healthier behaviour. Cohen et al. (1991) exposed 394 healthy volunteers to a controlled dose of a cold virus after measuring their psychological stress; higher stress predicted a dose-dependent rise in the rate of clinical colds — a rare experimental demonstration of the stress–immune pathway. And Marmot et al.'s Whitehall studies of British civil servants found a steep social gradient in cardiovascular disease and mortality: lower employment grade meant worse health even with healthcare access equal, implicating psychosocial factors such as low job control — a powerful sociocultural/situational determinant.

Command terms decide how far you must go. 'Explain' wants a detailed, evidenced account of a mechanism. 'Discuss' and 'Evaluate' want that PLUS developed critical thinking — weighing methodology, correlation versus causation, competing dispositional/situational explanations and cultural variation. For a 22-mark ERQ you are almost always in the second category, so plan your critical points before you write.
Evaluating the research and the model

items: **Correlation versus causation** — much of the evidence (optimism and recovery, SES and mortality) is correlational; a third variable such as SES can drive both the predictor and the outcome. Cohen's controlled-exposure and Marmot's prospective designs are stronger precisely because they narrow this gap.

**Construct measurement** — 'stress', 'optimism' and 'social support' are largely self-reported, and self-report is vulnerable to bias and to differing interpretations across individuals and cultures.

**Generalisability and sampling** — Scheier & Carver used only middle-aged men after one operation; Marmot studied British civil servants. Findings may not transfer across sex, age, culture or health condition.

**Dispositional/situational balance** — over-attributing outcomes to disposition ignores situation (Robins); over-attributing to situation ignores real biological vulnerability. The model's strength is that it demands both.

**Strength of the biopsychosocial model** — it is holistic, integrates the levels of analysis and justifies broader interventions; its weakness is that it is hard to test as a whole and can be so inclusive that it risks becoming unfalsifiable.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating the biopsychosocial model as additive** — writing 'biology plus social factors' without the INTERACTION. The interaction between the three factors is the whole idea; omit it and you have re-labelled the biomedical model.
**Confusing risk with protective factors, or forgetting direction** — say explicitly whether social support (protective) or social isolation (risk) is your point; naming the pole earns the mark.

**Mixing up dispositional and situational** — calling stress or availability a 'personality trait', or calling optimism an environmental factor. Internal-and-stable = dispositional; external-and-contextual = situational.

**Describing studies instead of USING them** — a full aim/procedure/findings with no sentence saying 'this shows that…'. Under our markband, description alone caps Criterion C; USE (linking the finding to the argument) is what reaches the top band.
**Relying on a single study** — one study, however detailed, caps Use of research. Bring two relevant studies you can genuinely use.
**Claiming causation from correlational data** — 'optimism causes health' overstates what correlational studies can show; state the limit and mention confounds such as SES.
**Ignoring the command term** — answering a 'Discuss' as if it were an 'Explain', with no developed critical thinking, forfeits Criterion D.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB Paper 2 markband (out of 22)

sections.question: Paper 2 ERQ: Discuss the biopsychosocial model of health, or determinants of health. [22 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model essay**

**Thesis (Focus).** The biopsychosocial model holds that health and illness emerge from the INTERACTION of biological, psychological and social determinants rather than from any single cause. This essay argues that the model's central claim — interaction — is well supported: I will show, using Marmot et al.'s Whitehall studies and Cohen et al.'s cold-virus experiment, that social and psychological determinants reliably reach into biological outcomes, while also weighing the model's methodological and conceptual limits. Throughout, 'discuss' means evaluating how strong that support really is.

**Knowledge and understanding.** The biomedical model treated illness as a purely biological malfunction and the mind as separate from the body. It explains acute infection well but poorly accounts for chronic, lifestyle-related disease, whose distribution depends on how people live and where they sit in society. The biopsychosocial model replaces it with three interacting domains: biological (genes, immune function, cortisol), psychological (stress appraisal, coping, self-efficacy) and social (socioeconomic status, social support, norms). Determinants within these domains act as risk factors (chronic stress, low SES, isolation) or protective factors (optimism, high SES, social support), and they influence health both directly and indirectly through health behaviours. The mechanism that makes 'interaction' concrete is the stress pathway: a psychosocial stressor activates the HPA axis, sustained cortisol suppresses immune function, and susceptibility to illness rises — a social cause producing a biological effect.

**Use of research — Study 1: Cohen et al. (1991).** *Aim:* to test whether psychological stress increases susceptibility to the common cold. *Procedure:* 394 healthy volunteers completed measures of psychological stress, were then given nasal drops containing a controlled low dose of a cold virus, and were quarantined and monitored for clinical colds. *Findings:* the rate of clinical colds rose in a dose-response fashion with higher psychological stress, even with viral exposure held constant. *Use:* because exposure was experimentally controlled and equal across participants, the difference in outcome cannot be attributed to differential exposure — it demonstrates that a PSYCHOLOGICAL determinant (stress) altered a BIOLOGICAL outcome (infection). This is direct evidence for the interaction the biopsychosocial model claims, and it does so with more causal force than correlational health data can.

**Use of research — Study 2: Marmot et al., Whitehall.** *Aim:* to investigate the social gradient in health among British civil servants. *Procedure:* large prospective cohorts of civil servants were followed over years, with health outcomes compared across employment grades. *Findings:* a steep gradient emerged — the lower the grade, the higher the rate of cardiovascular disease and mortality — and it persisted despite universal healthcare access, pointing to psychosocial factors such as low job control rather than access alone. *Use:* this shows a SOCIAL determinant (position in a status hierarchy) producing biological outcomes across an entire population, complementing Cohen: where Cohen isolates one psychobiological pathway experimentally, Marmot demonstrates the same logic operating socially and at scale. Together the two studies triangulate the model's core claim from different levels of analysis.

**Critical thinking (developed).** The support is strong but qualified. Cohen's experimental control is a genuine strength for causal inference, yet 'stress' was self-reported and the sample volunteered, limiting how far it generalises. Marmot's prospective design and huge sample are strengths, but as a naturalistic study it cannot fully exclude confounds — lifestyle behaviours correlate with grade, so the causal weight of 'job control' versus 'behaviour' is contestable, a classic correlation-versus-causation problem. There is also a dispositional/situational tension: one could argue lower-grade workers differ dispositionally, but Robins et al.'s finding that Vietnam veterans overwhelmingly stopped using heroin once their SITUATION changed warns against over-attributing health outcomes to stable personality. Finally, the biopsychosocial model itself is hard to falsify as a whole — its very inclusiveness, which makes it a strong framework for designing interventions, makes it weak as a testable hypothesis. The evidence therefore supports the model's DIRECTIONAL claim (psychosocial factors reach biology) more securely than it validates the model as a complete, testable theory.

**Conclusion.** The biopsychosocial model is well supported as an account of how determinants of health interact: Cohen shows the psychobiological pathway experimentally, Marmot shows it socially and at scale, and both defeat the biomedical assumption that biology alone decides health. Its limitations are real — correlational confounds, self-report, and low falsifiability — but they qualify rather than overturn the central claim. On balance, health is best understood not as a spark in isolation but as the outcome of biological, psychological and social factors in constant interaction.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 2 essay, out of 22):**

- **A — Focus on the question (2/2):** the thesis names the model, commits to an evaluative line ('well supported but qualified'), and reads 'discuss' correctly as weighing strength of support. Focus is maintained to the conclusion. *Top band: the question is addressed explicitly and sustained.*
- **B — Knowledge and understanding (6/6):** biomedical versus biopsychosocial, the three interacting domains, risk/protective factors, and the cortisol–immune mechanism are accurate, detailed and used with correct terminology. *Top band: knowledge is accurate and relevant with depth, not a list of definitions.*
- **C — Use of research (6/6):** TWO relevant studies, each with aim/procedure/findings, and — decisively — each explicitly USED ('this shows…', 'this demonstrates…') to support the argument, then triangulated against each other. *Top band: research is used to support, not merely described.*
- **D — Critical thinking (6/6):** developed evaluation — experimental control versus self-report, correlation versus causation, the dispositional/situational tension (with Robins), and the model's falsifiability — all linked back to the question rather than listed. *Top band: critical thinking is developed and relevant, not superficial.*
- **E — Clarity and organisation (2/2):** thesis-led structure, each study in its own developed paragraph, connectives that build an argument, and a conclusion that answers the question. *Top band: clearly organised and focused.*

**Why this reaches the top of every criterion:** it does the two things weaker essays skip — it makes the model's INTERACTION explicit, and it USES rather than describes its studies, then turns each limitation into evaluation tied to the question.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this ERQ, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same 22-mark Paper 2 markband, band by band, telling you where you landed and why. Watch the two CAPS it enforces — (1) use only ONE relevant study and Criterion C (Use of research) is capped below the top band; (2) DESCRIBE research without using it, or answer 'Discuss' with no developed evaluation, and Criterion D (Critical thinking) is capped. The habits that beat both caps: bring two studies, and after every finding write one sentence saying what it proves about your argument.
Where this leads

The determinants of health are the foundation of the whole option. Health problems (stress, addiction, obesity), health promotion, and the effectiveness of health-promotion programmes all return to the same question you have met here: which biological, psychological and social factors are in play, are they risk or protective, and is the behaviour better explained dispositionally or situationally? Master the interaction and the study-USE habit now, and the rest of the option is the same skill applied to new content.
